1: The three-year famine and David's inquiry of YHWH opens a series of appendices (2 Samuel 21-24) that the DH has gathered outside the strict chronological narrative. The connection of famine to bloodguilt is a standard ancient Near Eastern theological principle: corporate sin defiles the land and disrupts agricultural productivity (cf. the Achan episode, Josh 7; the Hittite Plague Prayers of Mursilis II, where the king inquires of divine oracles about what sin caused the plague afflicting his kingdom). YHWH's answer — 'there is bloodguilt on Saul and on his house, because he put the Gibeonites to death' — cites an offense not narrated elsewhere in the OT.
2: The Gibeonites were 'not Israelites but a remnant of the Amorites' — the same characterization as the Hittites, Hivites, and other Canaanite peoples who survived the conquest. The Josh 9 treaty with Gibeon was made by the Israelite leaders who 'did not inquire of the LORD' (Joshua 9:14) but were nonetheless legally binding because oaths were sacred. 'Saul had sought to kill them in his zeal for the people of Israel and Judah' — this Saulide pogrom against Gibeon is unnarrated but assumed; it may be connected to 1 Samuel 22:6-19 (Saul's massacre of the Nob priests, whom the Gibeonites may have been associated with).
3: David's question — 'What should I do for you? How shall I make atonement (kāpar) so that you will bless the inheritance of YHWH?' — uses the priestly vocabulary of expiation (kāpar, to cover/atone) in a non-sacrificial legal context. The 'inheritance of YHWH' (naḥălat YHWH) is the land of Israel — the land that belongs to YHWH and whose productivity depends on its purity (Leviticus 18:28: 'the land vomited out its inhabitants' because of their defilement). David is asking what legal act will restore covenant harmony and end the famine.
4: 'It is not a question of silver or gold between us and Saul or his house, and it is not for us to put any man to death in Israel.' The Gibeonites' initial response refuses two of the three standard reparation options in ancient Near Eastern law: monetary compensation and blood-feud (the wronged party taking their own revenge). The third option — execution by the authorized party, the king — is what they will request. Their framing of the issue as 'not our right to put to death' establishes that the execution they will request must be carried out by royal authority, not private vengeance.
5: The Gibeonites' description of Saul's offense — 'the man who destroyed us and planned to annihilate us so that we would have no place anywhere in Israel' — uses the vocabulary of genocidal intent (kālāh, to finish/annihilate; ʾăšer dimmāh lanû, who plotted against us). This is legal testimony, not merely complaint: they are establishing the seriousness of the violation that justifies their demand. The intent to eliminate a protected people from 'all the territory of Israel' constitutes the maximum violation of the covenant's protective obligations.
6: 'Let seven of his male descendants be handed over to us, so we may hang them before YHWH at Gibeah of Saul, at the mountain of YHWH.' The number seven (the complete set) and the location (Gibeah of Saul, Saul's own city) are both significant: seven is the covenant-number (the number of sacrifice and completeness); Gibeah of Saul as the execution site makes the judgment fall on Saul's own inheritance. The phrase 'before YHWH' (lĕYHWH) frames the execution as a judicial act with divine sanction — not murder but covenant-enforced execution of bloodguilt.
7: 'But the king spared Mephibosheth son of Jonathan son of Saul, because of the oath sworn before YHWH between David and Jonathan.' David's exemption of Mephibosheth invokes the Jonathan covenant (1 Samuel 18:3; 2 Samuel 20:42; 2 Samuel 23:18) as the legal basis for the exception. This is legally precise: the oath covered Jonathan's household, which includes Mephibosheth. The ḥesed obligation to Jonathan overrides the bloodguilt obligation to the Gibeonites — covenant loyalty takes precedence, and the specific covenant oath determines who falls within its protection.
8: Armoni and Mephibosheth (a different Mephibosheth from Jonathan's son — the son of Rizpah, Saul's concubine) plus the five sons of Merab (daughter of Saul) represent the seven required by the Gibeonites. The decision to include Merab's sons (through Adriel the Meholathite, 1 Samuel 18:19) extends the bloodguilt to descendants of Saul's daughter. The principle that corporate guilt passes through the male descendants of the offender is consistent with ANE legal traditions; the exclusion of Mephibosheth shows it is not absolute but subject to specific covenant exemptions.
9: 'He hanged them on the mountain before YHWH; all seven fell together.' The timing — 'at the beginning of harvest (bittĕḥillat qāṣîr), at the beginning of barley harvest' — links the execution to the agricultural calendar: the harvest season is exactly when rain is needed. The execution at the start of the harvest is therefore a statement of faith that YHWH will end the famine in response to the act of expiation. In ANE thought, ritual acts at critical agricultural moments were understood to affect the land's productivity.
10: Rizpah daughter of Aiah's vigil over the bodies — spreading sackcloth on a rock and keeping watch from the beginning of the barley harvest until the autumn rains fell — is one of the most powerful acts in the Samuel narrative. 'She did not let the birds of the sky touch them by day, or the wild animals by night' is the most minimal possible burial-proxy: she cannot bury them (forbidden during the period of expiation) but she can protect them from desecration. The sackcloth on the rock is both her mourning garment and her shelter for the months of vigil. Rizpah's sustained maternal grief has no parallel in the OT for duration and setting.
11: 'David was told what Rizpah daughter of Aiah, Saul's concubine, had done.' The narrative is explicit that it was Rizpah's action that prompted David's next response (vv12-14 — burying Saul's bones). The cause-and-effect here is deliberate: Rizpah's grief-vigil for the executed men catalyzes David's act of ḥesed toward the entire Saulide house. Rizpah, who has no political power, changes the king's actions through the moral weight of her sustained mourning. This is one of the DH's clearest examples of prophetic critique through narrative rather than oracle.
12: David retrieves the bones of Saul and Jonathan from Jabesh-gilead, where the men of Jabesh-gilead had secretly taken them after rescuing them from the Philistine walls of Beth-shan (1 Samuel 31:11-13). Jabesh-gilead's loyalty to Saul (they were the city he saved in his first military victory, 1 Sam 11) had extended beyond his death to the protection of his remains. David's retrieval acknowledges both Jabesh-gilead's faithfulness and his own obligation to Saul as YHWH's first anointed.
13: 'He brought up from there the bones of Saul and Jonathan, and they also gathered the bones of those who had been hanged' — the combining of Saul/Jonathan's bones with the seven executed men is the narrative's final act of reconciliation: the king who had violated the Gibeonite covenant and his seven punished descendants are all buried together with full honor. The gathering of the bones follows the principle that exposure of the dead is the maximum dishonor and that proper burial, even delayed, restores dignity.
14: 'They buried the bones of Saul and his son Jonathan in Zela of Benjamin, in the tomb of his father Kish.' The burial in Kish's tomb in Zela (a Benjaminite location, exact site uncertain) returns Saul and Jonathan to their tribal ancestral burial ground. 'After that, God answered prayer for the land' (wayyi''ātar ʾĕlōhîm lāʾāreṣ ʾaḥărê kēn) — the grammatically precise statement that divine response to prayer followed the burial confirms the theological principle: the bloodguilt that caused the famine has been atoned; the land can now receive the rains. The narrative closes the bloodguilt-famine-atonement cycle with formal divine response.
15: The transition to the Philistine war episodes (vv15-22) is marked by 'there was war again.' These episodes are thematically connected to the giant-killing tradition of 1 Sam 17 and functionally serve as closure for the Rephaim theme: the last descendants of the Anakim/Rephaim (the giant peoples of Canaan, Deuteronomy 2:11, 20; 2 Samuel 3:11) fall to David's servants. David's own near-death in battle (v17: he grows weary, and Ishbi-benob nearly kills him) is a final narrative mark of his mortality — the aging king can no longer fight as he once did.
16: Ishbi-benob is described as 'one of the descendants of the giants (yĕlîdê hārāpāh)' — the rāpāh (Raphah) being the ancestral giant figure associated with the Rephaim tradition (cf. 2 Samuel 21:18, 20, 22). His bronze spearhead weighing 300 shekels (~3.4 kg) is comparable to Goliath's iron spear with a 600-shekel head (1 Samuel 17:7) — the weaponry marks his class as superhuman opponent. The near-killing of David establishes the stakes: had Abishai not intervened, the Davidic line and its covenant would have been cut off.
17: 'Then David's men swore to him, “You must not go out to battle with us anymore, so that the lamp (nēr) of Israel will not be extinguished.”' The 'lamp of Israel' formula appears as a royal title in 1 Kings 11:36; 2 Samuel 15:4; 2 Kings 8:19 — the continuation of the Davidic line called a lamp that YHWH promised not to extinguish. Here it is used not as a promise about descendants but as a warning about David himself: he is the lamp, and his death in battle would extinguish Israel's light. The protective restriction placed on the king is the ANE convention of royal protection from front-line combat — the king's death in battle being potentially catastrophic for the army's morale and the covenant's continuity.
18: The four subsequent Philistine giant-battles (vv18-22) form a mini-catalog parallel to the Three/Thirty warriors of 2 Samuel 23. At Gob, Sibbecai the Hushathite kills Saph — the same Sibbechai listed as Mebunnai/Sibbechai in 23:27 and the monthly rotation list (1 Chronicles 27:11). The geographic detail of Gob is uncertain; the Chronicler's parallel (1 Chronicles 20:4) calls it Gezer.
19: 'Elhanan son of Jaare-oregim the Bethlehemite struck down Goliath the Gittite, whose spear shaft was like a weaver's rod.' The problem: 1 Sam 17 attributes Goliath's killing to David, while this verse attributes it to Elhanan. 1 Chronicles 20:5 'corrects' the text: 'Elhanan son of Jair struck down Lahmi the brother of Goliath.' The Chronicler's version attempts harmonization; the Samuel MT preserves the more difficult reading. Most scholarship holds that either: (1) there were two 'Goliaths' as a Philistine title; (2) the Elhanan tradition is older and the David story absorbed it; or (3) 'Elhanan' was another name for David (a throne name). The textual problem is among the most discussed in OT scholarship.
20: 'There was another battle at Gath, where there was a man of great size who had six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in all' — polydactyly as a marker of the giant class. Extra digits appear in ANE literature as a supernatural sign; in archaeological finds, polydactyly is a recessive genetic trait more common in isolated populations. The detail serves to characterize the Rephaim/giant class as physically anomalous from birth — their large size is not merely hereditary but marked by physical deviation from the human norm.
21: Jonathan son of Shimei (David's brother) kills the polydactyl giant of Gath. 'When he taunted Israel (ḥārēp), Jonathan son of Shimei struck him down' — the same taunting (ḥārap) vocabulary that Goliath used (1 Samuel 17:10, 25, 26, 36) and that Eleazar faced at Pas-dammim (2 Samuel 23:9). The giant's defiance of Israel is the standard provocation for the champion-battle tradition; David's nephew replays the Goliath dynamic at the family level. David's family defeating Goliath-class enemies is an inclusio: the Goliath tradition begins and ends with the house of Jesse.
22: 'These four were descendants of the giants in Gath (yĕlûdê hārāpāh bəgat), and they fell by the hand of David and his servants.' The summary closes the Rephaim theme: the last of the giant peoples are defeated by the Davidic court. The theological implication is that the conquest is now complete — the Rephaim/Anakim whom Joshua had reduced but not eliminated (Joshua 11:22: 'None of the Anakim was left in the land of the Israelites; only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod did some remain') are now wholly removed by David's warriors. The incomplete conquest is finished under the Davidic monarchy.
